rxn_class_summary: Cobalt-catalyzed electroreductive alkyl-alkyl bond formation by addition of unactivated primary, secondary, and tertiary alkyl chlorides to conjugated olefins (Michael acceptors) in an undivided cell with a sacrificial Zn anode.
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Co(II)(salen) to Co(I) at -1.7 V vs Fc/Fc+, which activates the alkyl chloride (by SN2 or XAA depending on substrate/electrolyte); second cathodic event at -2.1 V reduces either Co(III)(alkyl) intermediate or the alkyl-radical-derived adduct to an enolate; Zn (or stainless steel/Co) serves as sacrificial anode.

key_intermediates.name: Reduced [Co(I)(L1)]- (nucleophilic Co species, B); isolated as [Na+][Co(I)(L2)]-, X-ray characterized
Co(III)(alkyl) intermediate (C, complex 5, e.g. Co(L1)(n-octyl)) formed via SN2 with 1° RCl
Putative [Co(I)Cl(L1)]2- chloride-coordinated super-reduced species mediating XAA with 2°/3° RCl
α-Carbonyl radical adduct after radical addition to acrylate
Enolate (after cathodic reduction of α-carbonyl radical)
Polyacrylate oligomer (off-pathway in absence of proton source)

electron_flow: Cathode reduces Co(II)(L1) to nucleophilic Co(I), which activates R-Cl by SN2 (1° RCl, or 2°/3° in non-coordinating electrolyte) to give Co(III)(alkyl) that homolyzes to R•, or directly by halogen-atom abstraction (2°/3° RCl in LiCl) to give R• plus Co(III)-Cl. The R• adds to the conjugated alkene; the resulting α-EWG radical is reduced at the cathode (or by Co(I)) to an enolate that is protonated by n-BuOH to give product, while the Zn anode supplies the balancing oxidation.
rate_determining_step: C–Cl bond activation by Co(I) (SN2 vs XAA depending on substrate class and electrolyte); for 1° alkyl chlorides, subsequent Co–C bond homolysis of the stable Co(III)(alkyl) is the kinetically limiting step.

probe_reagents.name: 5-chloropent-1-ene (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Cyclized:uncyclized = 51%:12%, consistent with free alkyl radical intermediate after C–Cl activation.

probe_reagents.name: D2O (in place of n-BuOH proton source)
probe_reagents.observation: 65% yield with 95% D incorporation at the α-carbonyl position, supporting enolate protonation as the final step.

probe_reagents.name: Isolated Co(III)(alkyl) complex 5 (Co(L1)(n-C6H13))
probe_reagents.observation: Stoichiometric reaction with butyl acrylate gives only 28% of coupling product plus oligomers; CV shows reduction at -2.1 V in KPF6 but stable in LiCl, supporting SN2-derived Co(III)-alkyl pathway for 1° RCl.

probe_reagents.name: [Na+][Co(I)(L2)]- (chemically reduced Co(I))
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated and X-ray characterized; reacts immediately with 2-chlorooctane (color change Co(I) green → Co(III) red, full RCl consumption) — confirms Co(I) as the C–Cl activator.

probe_reagents.name: CV of Co(L1) ± RCl in KPF6 vs LiCl
probe_reagents.observation: In KPF6, 2° RCl gives ECE behavior with second wave at -2.1 V (Co(III)-alkyl reduction); in LiCl, only one catalytic-looking wave at -1.7 V with no Co(III)-alkyl wave, diagnostic of XAA pathway for 2°/3° RCl.

probe_reagents.name: UV-Vis of [Co(I)]- with added LiCl
probe_reagents.observation: No detectable change in E1/2 or speciation, so any chloride-bound super-reduced [Co(I)Cl]2- is invoked only as a minor but highly reactive species.

notes: Two mechanistic pathways for C–Cl activation operate in parallel and are substrate/electrolyte dependent: (i) SN2 by Co(I) giving a Co(III)(alkyl) that homolyzes (operative for 1° RCl in any electrolyte and for 2° RCl in non-coordinating PF6-/BF4-), and (ii) halogen atom abstraction (XAA) by Co(I) (or a chloride-bound [Co(I)Cl]2-) giving R• directly (operative for 3° RCl in all electrolytes and for 2° RCl in LiCl). The XAA pathway bypasses the slow Co–C homolysis step and gives higher yields, which guided ligand design (Co(L1) for 2°, Co(L3) for radical-clock substrates, Co(L5) for 3° RCl). A Zn-only (no current) background reaction contributes ~46% yield but is greatly enhanced by electrolysis with LiCl; acidic proton sources (HFIP) decompose catalyst, while n-BuOH or H2O prevent acrylate oligomerization by protonating the enolate.
